fuzzy-tool

Watch how a computer decides what a messy string probably meant. Pick a scenario, type something imperfect, and see how each fuzzy-matching method reasons its way to the likely correct value.

Reference list — what we're matching against

    The likely match

    How we got there

    N°1 Clean up the text

    Every entry goes through the same pipeline

    Before any scoring, your entry and every candidate are put through the same cleanup steps. Normalisation often matters more than which algorithm you choose.

    N°2 Compare against the top candidate

    The working, for the best match

    A summary of every method's score at the top of the card, with a visual explanation below showing how each method got there.

    N°3 The rest of the candidates

    Click any row to see the working

    Every other candidate in the reference list, ranked by best score. Expand a row to see the same per-method breakdown as above.

    What is fuzzy matching?

    Fuzzy matching is what an analyst reaches for when an exact match isn't enough — when typos, abbreviations, or reordered words stop two obviously-related strings from comparing equal. If your day-to-day involves deduplicating records, cleaning up a customer list, or joining free-text data against a reference table, this page covers where fuzzy matching fits in that work, what you need lined up before you run it, and how it actually decides what counts as a match.

    Fuzzy matching is how a computer figures out that two strings probably mean the same thing, even when they aren't identical. "Jhon Smith" almost certainly meant "John Smith". A human reads that instantly. A computer needs a small pipeline.

    In this chapter
    1. N°1 Where it sits — the five-step workflow
    2. N°2 Before you start — the analyst's checklist
    3. N°3 Inside Step 4 — the three stages of a fuzzy match
    N°1 Where it sits & when it runs

    The five-step workflow

    Data cleansing has three branches: validation, standardisation, and record linkage. Fuzzy matching lives inside record linkage — but it only fires when exact matching has already failed. Here is the whole pipeline as a single picture.

    Step Name What it does Example
    1 Validate Is the value sensible at all? Reject obviously broken records before you do anything else. Email contains @; postcode is 4 digits
    2 Standardise Force every record into the same format so later steps can compare like-for-like. JOHN SMITH → John Smith
    3 Exact match Is the value in the reference list verbatim? Cheap, fast, unambiguous — try this first. John Smith == John Smith ✓
    4 Fuzzy match this tool When exact fails, score similarity 0–1 against every candidate in the reference list. Jhon Smith → John Smith (0.94)
    5 Apply threshold Decide what the score means: auto-match, send to human review, or leave unmatched. 0.94 → auto-match

    Standardisation vs normalisation — they overlap in technique but live at different levels. Standardisation (Step 2 above) is the dataset-level practice: every phone number in our system should look the same. Normalisation is the per-string step inside Step 4, applied to a single value just before it gets compared. You'll see normalisation in the pipeline below.

    In practice You don't score against millions

    The diagram shows fuzzy match producing a score for every candidate in the reference list. That works for the 22 entries in this tool. It does not work for a CRM with a million customers — that would be a million similarity calculations per messy input.

    In production, fuzzy match runs as a two-step process inside Step 4:

    1. Candidate generation (a.k.a. blocking) — cheaply shortlist a few dozen candidates that could be a match. Common techniques: blocking keys (only compare records sharing the same surname initial or postcode prefix), trigram indexes (PostgreSQL pg_trgm, Elasticsearch), or locality-sensitive hashing (MinHash / LSH).
    2. Scoring — run the expensive similarity algorithms on the shortlist only.

    A good blocking strategy turns a million-row problem into a fifty-row problem before the similarity algorithm sees a single character. If you're working with a real-world reference list, look at libraries like recordlinkage, dedupe, or splink — they handle blocking and scoring as one pipeline.

    N°2 Before you start

    The analyst's checklist

    Six things you want lined up before you run the pipeline. Skipping any of them tends to show up later as a confusing result or a meeting about whether a match is "really" a match.

    # What you need Why Example
    1 Input strings The messy data you want to match. Can be one entry, a spreadsheet column, or a stream. Jhon Smith, 10 smith st
    2 Reference list The canonical, authoritative values you're matching against. Without this, there's nothing to match to. A customer master list, postcode register, product catalogue
    3 Knowledge of the noise What kinds of errors do you actually expect? Drives which algorithm earns its keep. Typos? Reordered words? Abbreviations? Phonetic variants?
    4 Normalisation rules Cleanups applied before any scoring. Must be identical on both sides — input and reference. Lowercase, strip punctuation, remove accents, expand St→Street
    5 Confidence thresholds Cutoffs for auto-match, human review, and unmatched. A business decision, not a technical one. Only needed when fuzzy match runs — exact match is unambiguous and doesn't use a threshold. ≥0.90 auto · 0.70–0.90 review · <0.70 unmatched
    6 A review process Where flagged matches go, who looks at them, how fast, and where the final answer is recorded. Queue in a sheet → reviewed daily → outcome written back to source
    7 A candidate-generation strategy Only matters if the reference list is large. You can't realistically score one input against a million records — you need a way to shortlist first. See the candidate-generation note in the workflow above. Block by surname initial, postcode prefix, or trigram index
    N°3 Inside Step 4 — fuzzy match

    The three stages of a fuzzy match

    Zooming into Step 4 from the workflow above. Three explicit stages run in order, every time. The same worked example flows through all three so you can watch the data transform.

    The tool shows this pipeline in action — type a messy entry and watch each stage play out. The algorithms page covers what happens inside Stage 2: the four comparison techniques and when each one earns its keep.

    The algorithms

    Plain-English explanations of the four similarity-scoring algorithms used inside Stage 2 of the fuzzy-matching pipeline. No code, no math — just the intuition and when each one earns its keep.

    Why more than one algorithm?

    There's no single "best" similarity score. Each algorithm measures a different kind of similarity. A name with a typo needs one approach; a sentence with words reordered needs another; a partial match inside a longer string needs a third. The skill is picking the right tool — or combining several — for the noise you actually have.

    The four algorithms below all sit inside Stage 2 of the pipeline on the concepts page. They assume the strings have already been normalised.

    In this chapter
    1. N°1 Levenshtein
    2. N°2 Jaro-Winkler
    3. N°3 Jaccard similarity (tokens)
    4. N°4 Jaccard similarity (n-grams)
    N°1 Method

    Levenshtein

    Edit distance

    Count the fewest single-character edits — insertions, deletions, and substitutions — needed to turn one string into the other.

    Analogy

    Think of it as counting keystrokes. How many characters would you have to change to fix a typo? That's the distance. Divide by the length of the longer string to turn the count into a 0-to-1 similarity score.

    Worked example
    kitten vs sitting — substitutions in orange, the trailing insert in blue.
    When to use
    • Typos in short-to-medium strings (names, product codes).
    • Spelling variations of the same word in the same language.
    • When every character matters and position is meaningful.
    When it fails
    • Word reordering ("John Smith" vs "Smith, John" scores near zero).
    • Very different string lengths — many edits even if the content is similar.
    • Phonetic differences ("Catherine" vs "Katherine" starts losing ground fast).
    In your own project

    Python: rapidfuzz (fast) or python-Levenshtein. JavaScript: fastest-levenshtein. Almost every language has a well-tested implementation — you shouldn't write your own unless you're learning.

    N°2 Method

    Jaro-Winkler

    Edit distance (specialized)

    Like Levenshtein, but purpose-built for short strings like names, and weighted to reward pairs that start with the same characters.

    Analogy

    People typo the ends of names more often than the beginnings. Jaro-Winkler bakes this into the score: strings that share a prefix get a bonus. "Jonathan" and "Jonathon" score higher than a character count alone would predict, because the first five letters match.

    Worked example
    jonathan vs jonathon — matched characters in green, the shared prefix underlined for the Winkler bonus.
    When to use
    • Short strings where order matters: names, product SKUs, place names.
    • When you want to weight "same start" as evidence of a match.
    • Record-linkage tasks (deduplicating customer or patient records).
    When it fails
    • Long strings like sentences — the prefix weighting stops helping.
    • Word reordering — same weakness as Levenshtein.
    • Phonetic variations — combine with a phonetic method if you need those.
    In your own project

    Python: jellyfish, rapidfuzz. JavaScript: natural, string-similarity-js.

    N°3 Method

    Jaccard similarity (tokens)

    Set-based

    Treat each string as a bag of words. Similarity = how many words they share, divided by the total number of unique words across both.

    Analogy

    Ignore word order entirely. "John Smith" and "Smith, John" contain the same words, so they're the same to this method. Great when human data entry scrambles the order but keeps the content.

    Worked example
    red blue green vs green red yellow — shared words on the left, words unique to each string on the right.
    When to use
    • Text where word order varies but the words themselves carry the meaning.
    • Short notes, tags, keyword lists, concatenated name fields.
    • As one input when combining multiple methods.
    When it fails
    • Typos within words — "apple" and "appel" are totally different tokens.
    • Single-word strings — the set has one element, so it's all-or-nothing.
    • Different vocabularies that mean the same thing — "car" vs "automobile".
    In your own project

    A five-line function in any language. For large-scale near-duplicate detection, look up MinHash / LSH.

    N°4 Method

    Jaccard similarity (n-grams)

    Set-based

    Same idea as Jaccard tokens, but instead of whole words you use short overlapping character sequences. Catches similarity inside words.

    Analogy

    Slide a small window across the string and collect every length-n chunk. "Street" and "St" share the 2-gram "St" — enough to catch a partial match. This is what powers a lot of "typo-tolerant" search under the hood.

    Worked example
    hello vs hallo with 2-grams. The shared character pairs are the overlap that drives the score.
    When to use
    • Fuzzy search and autocomplete.
    • Finding near-duplicate text blocks.
    • Detecting partial matches inside long strings.
    When it fails
    • Very short strings — a 2-letter string has only one 2-gram.
    • Totally different vocabularies — shares no n-grams at all.
    • When position matters. Order of n-grams is lost.
    In your own project

    Easy to write by hand, or look for ngram utilities in your language's standard library. For big-scale search, inverted indexes over n-grams are a classic trick.

    Combining algorithms in practice

    Real pipelines often run several algorithms at once and either take the highest score, a weighted sum, or feed all the scores into a classifier. The tool shows every algorithm's score side-by-side for each candidate, so you can see how they agree and disagree on the same pair of strings.

    For when each score crosses into "auto-match" or "needs human review", see the threshold cascade on the concepts page.

    Jennifer Payne — data scientist

    Making the complex simple, one tech project at a time.
